title: Media representations of class, gender, ethnicity, and age groups
summary: 9699 — stereotypes, counter-types, and ideological representations in media content.
learningObjectives: Explain how class, gender, ethnicity, and age are portrayed.
Apply content analysis to media examples.

simpleExplanation.title: What the media shows — and hides
simpleExplanation.summary: Media often reinforce stereotypes but can also challenge them; representation shapes how groups are seen and see themselves.

flashcards.back: Simplified, exaggerated group image repeated across media.
Connell — dominant ideal of tough, heterosexual masculinity in media.
Media filmed for male viewer — women objectified.
Trowler — groups ignored or trivialised in media.
Deliberate challenge to stereotypes — diversity casting, own-voice media.

sections.content: **Media representations** (10.1) links **1.3 identity** to **9.2 theory**. Use **Trowler**, **Van Zoonen**, **Hooks**. Apply **content analysis** method from Paper 1.

sections.question: Assess the view that the media stereotype ethnic minorities. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**Stereotype evidence:** **Cottle** — news frames minorities as **problem** (crime, immigration); **under-representation** in drama; **terrorism** linked to Islam in headlines; **victim/threat** dual stereotypes.

**Counter-evidence:** **Increasing diversity** on screen; **own-voice** creators (YouTube, streaming); **anti-racist** campaigns; **complex** characters in some productions.

**Effects debate:** **Hall** — representation shapes **identity and racism**; **active audience** may reject stereotypes.

**Evaluation:** **Historical and ongoing** stereotyping well documented — **progress uneven** by platform and genre.

**Conclusion:** Stereotypes **still common** but **contested** more than in past.
